title: Count greater elements to the left
description: Count elements greater than current on the left side

examples.explanation:
Array: [3, 2, 1]
Step 1: i=0 (element 3)
  Left elements: none
  Count: 0
  Result so far: [0]
Step 2: i=1 (element 2)
  Left elements: [3]
  Compare 3 > 2: true → count=1
  Count: 1
  Result so far: [0, 1]
Step 3: i=2 (element 1)
  Left elements: [3, 2]
  Compare 3 > 1: true → count=1
  Compare 2 > 1: true → count=2
  Count: 2
  Final result: [0, 1, 2]

Array: [1, 2, 3, 4]
Step 1: i=0 (element 1)
  Left elements: none
  Count: 0
  Result: [0]
Step 2: i=1 (element 2)
  Left elements: [1]
  Compare 1 > 2: false → count=0
  Result: [0, 0]
Step 3: i=2 (element 3)
  Left elements: [1, 2]
  Compare 1 > 3: false
  Compare 2 > 3: false → count=0
  Result: [0, 0, 0]
Step 4: i=3 (element 4)
  Left elements: [1, 2, 3]
  Compare 1 > 4: false
  Compare 2 > 4: false
  Compare 3 > 4: false → count=0
  Final result: [0, 0, 0, 0]

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: For each element, iterate through all previous elements to count how many are greater. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Greater Elements to the Left Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting elements greater than current on the left side using nested loops.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the problem by directly implementing the definition: for each element in the array, we examine all elements to its left and count how many are greater. This is intuitive because it mirrors how a human would solve the problem manually - looking at each position and comparing with previous positions. The algorithm works by using two nested loops: an outer loop iterates through each element (from left to right), and an inner loop iterates through all elements to the left of the current element. For each comparison where the left element is greater than the current element, we increment a counter. This counter is then stored in the result array. The time complexity is O(n²) because for an array of size n, we perform approximately n*(n-1)/2 comparisons in the worst case. The space complexity is O(n) for the output array, but if we consider only auxiliary space (excluding output), it's O(1) since we only use a few variables. This approach connects directly to the problem statement - it's the most straightforward implementation of what's being asked. Common mistakes include: 1) Starting the inner loop from the wrong index (should be from 0 to i-1), 2) Forgetting to reset the counter for each element, 3) Confusing greater-than with greater-than-or-equal-to (the problem specifies 'greater than'), 4) Not handling edge cases like empty arrays or single-element arrays. The real code pattern involves initializing a result array, then for each index i from 0 to n-1, initialize count=0, then for each index j from 0 to i-1, if arr[j] > arr[i], increment count, then append count to result.

examples.trace:
Array: [3, 2, 1]
Step 1: i=0 (element 3)
  Left elements: none
  Count: 0
  Result so far: [0]
Step 2: i=1 (element 2)
  Left elements: [3]
  Compare 3 > 2: true → count=1
  Count: 1
  Result so far: [0, 1]
Step 3: i=2 (element 1)
  Left elements: [3, 2]
  Compare 3 > 1: true → count=1
  Compare 2 > 1: true → count=2
  Count: 2
  Final result: [0, 1, 2]

Array: [1, 2, 3, 4]
Step 1: i=0 (element 1)
  Left elements: none
  Count: 0
  Result: [0]
Step 2: i=1 (element 2)
  Left elements: [1]
  Compare 1 > 2: false → count=0
  Result: [0, 0]
Step 3: i=2 (element 3)
  Left elements: [1, 2]
  Compare 1 > 3: false
  Compare 2 > 3: false → count=0
  Result: [0, 0, 0]
Step 4: i=3 (element 4)
  Left elements: [1, 2, 3]
  Compare 1 > 4: false
  Compare 2 > 4: false
  Compare 3 > 4: false → count=0
  Final result: [0, 0, 0, 0]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Counting Greater Elements for a Single Position
ladders.desc: Given an array and an index, count how many elements to the left are greater than the element at that index.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force solution - it solves the problem for a single position rather than the entire array. The intuition is simple: to know how many previous elements are greater than the current element, we need to examine each previous element one by one and check if it's greater. This works because we're directly implementing the comparison logic for one specific position. The algorithm: 1) Accept an array and an index i as input, 2) Initialize a counter to 0, 3) Iterate through indices j from 0 to i-1, 4) For each j, if array[j] > array[i], increment the counter, 5) Return the counter. Time complexity is O(i) which in the worst case (when i=n-1) is O(n). Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a counter variable. This feeds into the full problem (L0) because the full solution simply applies this sub-routine to every position in the array. Common mistakes: 1) Including the current element in the comparison (should only compare with elements at indices < i), 2) Using >= instead of > (the problem asks for 'greater than', not 'greater than or equal to'), 3) Not handling the case when i=0 (no left elements, should return 0). The code pattern is straightforward: define a function that takes array and i, then loop j from 0 to i-1, comparing and counting.

examples.trace:
Array: [5, 3, 8, 1], i=3 (element 1)
Initialize count = 0
j=0: array[0]=5 > array[3]=1 → true → count=1
j=1: array[1]=3 > 1 → true → count=2
j=2: array[2]=8 > 1 → true → count=3
Return count=3

Array: [2, 4, 6, 8], i=2 (element 6)
Initialize count = 0
j=0: array[0]=2 > array[2]=6 → false
j=1: array[1]=4 > 6 → false
Return count=0

ladders.title: Core Logic — Comparing Each Element with All Previous Elements
ladders.desc: The fundamental pattern of nested iteration where each element is compared with all elements before it.
ladders.explanation: This core logic represents the algorithmic pattern at the heart of the brute force approach: for each position in a sequence, examine all preceding positions. The intuition is that to understand relationships between an element and everything that came before it, we need to check each previous element individually. This pattern works because it's exhaustive - it doesn't miss any comparisons. The algorithm involves two loops: an outer loop that moves through each element (from first to last), and an inner loop that goes through all elements before the current outer element. For the 'count greater elements' problem, we add a comparison check inside the inner loop. Time complexity is O(n²) because for an array of n elements, the inner loop runs approximately n/2 times on average for each outer iteration, leading to roughly n²/2 total operations. Space complexity is O(1) for the comparison operations themselves (excluding any output storage). This feeds into L1 (the sub-routine) because L1 implements this pattern for a specific index. Common mistakes: 1) Having the inner loop go from 0 to n-1 instead of 0 to i-1 (which would compare with all elements including future ones), 2) Not resetting counters between outer iterations, 3) Confusing the loop indices. The code pattern is: for i in range(n): for j in range(i): # compare element at j with element at i.

examples.trace:
Array indices: 0=A, 1=B, 2=C
Outer i=0: no inner loop (j from 0 to -1)
Outer i=1: inner j=0 → compare A with B
Outer i=2: inner j=0 → compare A with C
           inner j=1 → compare B with C
Total comparisons: A-B, A-C, B-C

Indices: 0=X, 1=Y, 2=Z, 3=W
i=1: j=0 → X-Y
i=2: j=0 → X-Z, j=1 → Y-Z
i=3: j=0 → X-W, j=1 → Y-W, j=2 → Z-W
Total: 6 comparisons

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through Array Elements with Indices
ladders.desc: Accessing each element in an array using index-based loops.
ladders.explanation: This building block is about systematically visiting each element in an array using indices. The intuition is that arrays are indexed collections, so to process all elements we need to access each position sequentially. This works because arrays provide constant-time access to any element given its index. The algorithm: initialize an index variable (usually i) to 0, then while i is less than the array length, access array[i], perform some operation, then increment i. In pseudocode: for i from 0 to length(array)-1: process array[i]. Time complexity is O(n) for visiting n elements. Space complexity is O(1) for the index variable. This feeds into L2 (core logic) because L2 requires iterating through all elements (outer loop) and also through subsets of elements (inner loop). Common mistakes: 1) Using <= instead of < for the loop condition (would cause index out of bounds), 2) Forgetting to increment the index (infinite loop in while loops), 3) Starting from 1 instead of 0 (missing first element). The code pattern is fundamental: for i in range(len(arr)): # use arr[i].

examples.trace:
Array length = 3
i=0: access arr[0]=10
i=1: access arr[1]=20
i=2: access arr[2]=30
Done: visited all 3 elements

Length = 4
i=0: arr[0]=5
i=1: arr[1]=3
i=2: arr[2]=7
i=3: arr[3]=1
All 4 elements visited

testCases.expected: Visited: a, b, c, d, e

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Determining if one number is greater than another.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is the fundamental comparison that drives the entire algorithm. The intuition is simple: to count 'greater' elements, we need to be able to determine when one number is larger than another. This works because numbers have a well-defined ordering - for any two numbers a and b, either a > b, a < b, or a = b. The algorithm: given two numbers a and b, evaluate the expression a > b. This returns a boolean value (true or false). Time complexity is O(1) - it's a single comparison operation. Space complexity is O(1) - no additional memory needed. This feeds into L3 (array iteration) because when we iterate through arrays, we often need to compare elements. Common mistakes: 1) Confusing > with >= (greater than vs greater than or equal to), 2) Not understanding that comparisons work differently for different data types (strings compare lexicographically), 3) For floating-point numbers, precision issues might affect comparisons. The code pattern is simply: if a > b: # do something.

examples.trace: Is 5 > 3? Yes, so result is true
Is 2 > 7? No, so result is false

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Fundamental concept of ordered collections accessible by position.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces arrays as ordered collections where each element has a position (index). The intuition is that arrays allow us to store multiple values together and access them by their position. This works because arrays allocate contiguous memory locations, with the first element at index 0, second at index 1, etc. The key operations: 1) Accessing an element at a specific index (e.g., arr[0] gets the first element), 2) Knowing the length/size of the array. Time complexity for access is O(1) - constant time. Space complexity for the array itself is O(n) where n is the number of elements. This feeds into L4 (comparisons) because once we can access array elements, we can compare them. Common beginner mistakes: 1) Thinking arrays start at index 1 (they start at 0 in most languages), 2) Accessing indices beyond the array bounds (causing errors), 3) Confusing array values with array indices. The concept is essential because the brute force solution relies entirely on accessing array elements by their indices to make comparisons.

examples.trace:
Array has 3 elements at positions 0, 1, 2
Index 0: value 10
Index 1: value 20 ← we want this
Index 2: value 30
So arr[1] = 20

Count the elements:
Position 0: 5
Position 1: 3
Position 2: 7
Position 3: 1
Total elements = 4

testCases.input: Array: [a, b, c, d], How many elements?

approaches.name: Self-Balancing BST O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Traverse the array from left to right, inserting each element into a self-balancing BST (like AVL or Red-Black Tree) and querying the count of greater elements using tree properties. This maintains order dynamically.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Greater Elements to Left Using Self-Balancing BST
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting elements greater than current on left side using AVL tree insertion and querying.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting for each element how many elements to its left are greater than it. The self-balancing BST approach works by maintaining a dynamic sorted structure as we traverse left to right. For each element, we query the tree to count how many existing elements are greater (which are all to the left since we process left to right), then insert the element. The key insight is that a self-balancing BST (like AVL) maintains O(log n) operations while storing elements in sorted order. For counting greater elements, we can augment each node with the size of its right subtree (elements greater than it) plus recursively count greater elements during traversal. Intuition: As we insert elements in order of appearance, the tree represents all previously seen elements sorted. When querying for a new element, we traverse the tree: if current node value ≤ query value, all greater elements are in right subtree, so we add right subtree size and recurse right; if current node value > query value, the current node itself is greater plus all in its right subtree, so we add (1 + right subtree size) and recurse left to check for more greater elements. This yields O(log n) query time. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize empty AVL tree with size augmentation. 2. Initialize result array of same length as input. 3. For each element arr[i] from left to right: a. Query tree for count of elements greater than arr[i] using recursive traversal that sums right subtree sizes when node values > query. b. Store count in result[i]. c. Insert arr[i] into AVL tree with balancing and size updates. 4. Return result array. Time: O(n log n) for n insertions and queries. Space: O(n) for tree and result. Connection: This integrates all lower-level components into complete solution. Common mistakes: Forgetting to update subtree sizes during rotations, incorrect greater-than logic in query, not handling duplicates properly (greater means strictly greater, so for duplicates, elements equal don't count). Real code pattern: Define Node with value, left, right, height, size. Insert with standard AVL insertion plus size updates. Query function count_greater(root, val): if root is None: return 0; if root.val <= val: return count_greater(root.right, val); else: return 1 + root.right.size + count_greater(root.left, val).

examples.trace: Initialize tree empty, result array length 3. i=0, arr[0]=3: query tree (empty) -> count=0, result[0]=0, insert 3 into tree (root=3, size=1). i=1, arr[1]=2: query tree: root=3 > 2, so count = 1 + right.size(0) + query left(None) = 1, result[1]=1, insert 2 into tree (balance if needed, sizes updated). i=2, arr[2]=1: query tree: root=2 > 1, count = 1 + right.size(0) + query left: left is 3? Wait tree after insertions: Actually after inserting 2, tree might be balanced. Let's trace precisely: After i=0: tree: 3 (size=1). i=1: query: root=3 > 2 -> count=1 + right.size(0)=1, result[1]=1. Insert 2: tree becomes 3 with left child 2 (size updates: 3 size=2, 2 size=1). i=2: query: root=3 > 1 -> count=1 + right.size(0)=1 + query left: left is 2, 2 > 1 -> count adds 1 + right.size(0)=1, total count=1+1=2, result[2]=2. So output [0,1,2].

Initialize tree empty. i=0, arr[0]=1: query empty -> 0, result[0]=0, insert 1. i=1, arr[1]=2: query tree: root=1 <= 2, so go right (None) -> count=0, result[1]=0, insert 2 (tree: 1 with right child 2). i=2, arr[2]=3: query: root=1 <= 3 -> go right to 2, 2 <= 3 -> go right None -> count=0, result[2]=0, insert 3. i=3, arr[3]=4: query: root=1 <=4 -> right to 2, 2<=4 -> right to 3, 3<=4 -> right None -> count=0, result[3]=0. Output [0,0,0,0].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Query Count of Greater Elements in Augmented BST
ladders.desc: Function to count nodes with values greater than given value in a size-augmented BST.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core query operation needed for the full problem. Given a BST where each node stores the size of its subtree (number of nodes in that subtree), we need to count how many nodes have values strictly greater than a query value. The intuition leverages BST property: left subtree contains smaller values, right subtree contains larger values. By augmenting with subtree sizes, we can compute counts without traversing entire tree. Algorithm: Recursively traverse from root. At each node: if node.value <= query value, then all greater elements must be in right subtree (since left subtree has smaller or equal values), so we recurse to right child. If node.value > query value, then this node is greater, plus all nodes in its right subtree are greater (because all values in right subtree are > node.value > query value), so we add (1 + size of right subtree) to our count, and recurse to left child to check for more greater nodes there. Base case: if node is None, return 0. Time: O(log n) average for balanced BST, O(n) worst for skewed. Space: O(log n) for recursion stack. Connection: This function is called for each element in the array in L0. Common mistakes: Using >= instead of > (problem says greater, not greater or equal), forgetting to add right subtree size efficiently, not handling duplicates correctly (equal values don't count as greater). Real code pattern: def count_greater(node, val): if not node: return 0; if node.val <= val: return count_greater(node.right, val); else: return 1 + (node.right.size if node.right else 0) + count_greater(node.left, val).

examples.input: Tree: root=5 (size=3, left=3 size=1, right=7 size=1), query=4
examples.trace: Start at root 5: 5 > 4, so count = 1 + right.size (1) = 2, recurse left to 3. At node 3: 3 <= 4, so recurse right (None) -> returns 0. Total count = 2. Greater nodes: 5 and 7.

examples.input: Tree: root=2 (size=2, left=1 size=1, right=None), query=3
examples.trace: Start at root 2: 2 <= 3, so recurse right (None) -> returns 0. No nodes greater than 3.

testCases.input: Tree: root=8 (size=3, left=4 size=1, right=12 size=1), query=6
Tree: root=8 (size=3, left=4 size=1, right=12 size=1), query=10
Tree: root=6 (size=5, left=2 size=2 (left=1, right=3), right=9 size=2 (left=7, right=10)), query=5
Tree: root=6 (size=5, left=2 size=2, right=9 size=2), query=8
Tree: root=3 (size=4, left=1 size=1, right=5 size=2 (left=4, right=6)), query=2
Tree: root=3 (size=4, left=1 size=1, right=5 size=2), query=7
Tree: root=1 (size=3, left=None, right=2 size=2 (right=3)), query=0

ladders.title: Core Logic — Augmenting BST with Subtree Size for Efficient Queries
ladders.desc: Technique to store subtree size in each node to enable O(log n) count queries.
ladders.explanation: The core technique that makes the self-balancing BST approach efficient is augmenting each node with the size of its subtree (number of nodes in that subtree). This allows us to answer queries like 'how many nodes are greater than a value' without traversing entire subtrees. Intuition: In a BST, for any node, all nodes in its right subtree have greater values. If we know the size of the right subtree instantly (via augmentation), we can add that to our count when we know the node is greater than query value. Similarly, we use recursion to navigate left/right based on comparisons. This reduces query time from O(n) to O(log n) in balanced trees. The augmentation must be maintained during insertions, deletions, and rotations. Algorithm: Define Node with attributes: value, left, right, size. Size is computed as: size = 1 + left.size + right.size. During insertion: after inserting a node, update sizes along the path from new node to root. During rotations (for balancing), recalculate sizes for affected nodes. For querying, as in L1, we use size to quickly add right subtree counts. Time: O(log n) per update/query if tree balanced. Space: O(1) extra per node for size. Connection: This augmentation enables the L1 query function. Common mistakes: Forgetting to update sizes after rotations, incorrect size initialization (should be 1 for new node), not propagating size updates up the tree. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None; self.size = 1. Update size during insertion: after recursive insert, node.size = 1 + size(node.left) + size(node.right).

examples.input: Insert values [5, 3, 7] into empty augmented BST
examples.output: Tree with sizes: root=5 size=3, left=3 size=1, right=7 size=1
examples.trace: Start empty. Insert 5: create node(5) size=1. Insert 3: compare 3<5, insert as left child of 5. Update 5 size: 1 + left.size(1) + right.size(0) = 2. But wait, after inserting 3, node 3 size=1, then update parent 5: size = 1 + 1 + 0 = 2. Insert 7: 7>5, insert as right child of 5. Update 5 size: 1 + left.size(1) + right.size(1) = 3. Node 7 size=1. Final: 5(size=3), left=3(size=1), right=7(size=1).

Insert 4: root size=1. Insert 2: 2<4, left child, update 4 size=2. Insert 6: 6>4, right child, update 4 size=3. Insert 1: 1<4, go left to 2, 1<2, left child of 2, update 2 size=2, update 4 size=4. Insert 3: 3<4, go left to 2, 3>2, right child of 2, update 2 size=3, update 4 size=5. Insert 5: 5>4, go right to 6, 5<6, left child of 6, update 6 size=2, update 4 size=6. Insert 7: 7>4, go right to 6, 7>6, right child of 6, update 6 size=3, update 4 size=7. Final sizes: root 4 size=7, left 2 size=3, right 6 size=3, etc.

testCases.input: Insert [10] into empty tree, get size of root
Insert [5, 3] into empty, get size of root 5
Insert [8, 4, 12] into empty, get size of root 8
Insert [1, 2, 3] into empty, get size of root 1
Insert [7, 3, 10, 1, 5] into empty, get size of root 7
Insert [6, 2, 8, 1, 4, 7, 9] into empty, get size of root 6
Insert [9, 5, 13, 3, 7, 11, 15] into empty, get size of root 9
Insert [2, 1, 3] into empty, get size of node 1
Insert [4, 2, 6, 1, 3, 5, 7] into empty, get size of node 2
Insert [100, 50, 150] into empty, get size of node 150

ladders.title: Building Block — Self-Balancing BST Insertion with Rotations (AVL)
ladders.desc: Inserting a value into an AVL tree while maintaining balance via rotations.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential because the efficiency of the full solution relies on a balanced BST to guarantee O(log n) operations. An AVL tree is a self-balancing BST where the heights of left and right subtrees of any node differ by at most 1. Insertion follows standard BST insertion, then updates heights and performs rotations if balance factor becomes ±2. Rotations rebalance the tree without breaking BST property. Intuition: After insertion, we traverse back up to root, updating heights. If at any node balance factor (left height - right height) is 2 or -2, we identify one of four cases: left-left, left-right, right-right, right-left, and perform appropriate rotations (single or double) to restore balance. This ensures tree remains approximately balanced, giving O(log n) height. Algorithm: 1. Perform standard BST recursive insertion. 2. Update height of current node: height = 1 + max(left.height, right.height). 3. Get balance factor = left.height - right.height. 4. If balance > 1: left-heavy. If value < node.left.value: left-left case, right rotate. Else: left-right case, left rotate on left child then right rotate. 5. If balance < -1: right-heavy. If value > node.right.value: right-right case, left rotate. Else: right-left case, right rotate on right child then left rotate. 6. Return node. Time: O(log n) for insertion and balancing. Space: O(log n) recursion. Connection: This insertion maintains tree balance for L2 augmentation. Common mistakes: Forgetting to update heights after rotations, incorrect rotation cases, not handling empty children properly. Real code pattern: def insert(node, val): if not node: return Node(val); if val < node.val: node.left = insert(node.left, val); else: node.right = insert(node.right, val); node.height = 1 + max(height(node.left), height(node.right)); balance = get_balance(node); if balance > 1 and val < node.left.val: return right_rotate(node); if balance > 1 and val > node.left.val: node.left = left_rotate(node.left); return right_rotate(node); if balance < -1 and val > node.right.val: return left_rotate(node); if balance < -1 and val < node.right.val: node.right = right_rotate(node.right); return left_rotate(node); return node.

examples.input: Insert 3 into empty AVL, then insert 2, then insert 1
examples.trace: Start empty. Insert 3: root=3 height=1. Insert 2: 2<3, insert as left of 3. Update heights: 2 height=1, 3 height=2. Balance at 3: left height 1 - right height 0 = 1, okay. Insert 1: 1<3, go left to 2, 1<2, insert as left of 2. Update heights: 1 height=1, 2 height=2, 3 height=3. Check balance at 2: left height 1 - right height 0 = 1, okay. Check balance at 3: left height 2 - right height 0 = 2, unbalanced left-left case (since 1<2). Perform right rotation on 3: make 2 new root, 3 as right child of 2, 2's right child becomes left child of 3? Actually: right rotation at 3: left child 2 becomes new root, 3 becomes right child of 2, original right subtree of 2 (None) becomes left child of 3. Update heights: 1 height=1, 3 height=1, 2 height=2. Balanced.

examples.input: Insert 1 into empty, then insert 2, then insert 3
examples.trace: Insert 1: root=1 height=1. Insert 2: 2>1, insert as right of 1. Heights: 2 height=1, 1 height=2. Balance at 1: left height 0 - right height 1 = -1, okay. Insert 3: 3>1, go right to 2, 3>2, insert as right of 2. Update heights: 3 height=1, 2 height=2, 1 height=3. Check balance at 2: left height 0 - right height 1 = -1, okay. Check balance at 1: left height 0 - right height 2 = -2, unbalanced right-right case. Perform left rotation on 1: make 2 new root, 1 as left child of 2, 2's left child (None) becomes right child of 1. Heights: 1 height=1, 3 height=1, 2 height=2. Balanced.

testCases.input: Insert [5] into empty, get height of root
Insert [5, 3] into empty, get height of root
Insert [5, 3, 7] into empty, get height of root
Insert [10, 5, 15, 2] into empty, get height of root
Insert [10, 5, 15, 2, 7] into empty, get height of root
Insert [30, 20, 40, 10, 25] into empty, get height of root
Insert [30, 20, 40, 10, 25, 35, 50] into empty, get height of root
Insert [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] into empty, get height of root
Insert [5, 4, 3, 2, 1] into empty, get height of root
Insert [8, 4, 12, 2, 6, 10, 14] into empty, get height of root

ladders.title: Basic Operation — BST Insertion and In-Order Traversal
ladders.desc: Inserting a value into a binary search tree and traversing in sorted order.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is the basis for self-balancing trees. A binary search tree (BST) is a tree where for each node, all values in left subtree are less, and all in right subtree are greater. Insertion adds a new node while preserving this property. In-order traversal visits left subtree, then node, then right subtree, yielding values in sorted order. Intuition: BST organizes data dynamically so search, insertion, deletion can be efficient if tree is balanced. Insertion: start at root, compare value with current node; if less, go left; if greater or equal, go right; repeat until empty spot, insert new node there. In-order traversal uses recursion or stack to visit nodes in ascending order. This is foundational because self-balancing BSTs build upon BST properties and use traversals for debugging. Algorithm for insertion: def insert(root, val): if root is None: return Node(val); if val < root.val: root.left = insert(root.left, val); else: root.right = insert(root.right, val); return root. In-order traversal: def inorder(root): if root: inorder(root.left); print(root.val); inorder(root.right). Time: Insertion O(h) where h is height, O(log n) if balanced, O(n) if skewed. Traversal O(n). Space: O(h) for recursion. Connection: This operation is used in L3 for AVL insertion before balancing. Common mistakes: Inserting duplicates incorrectly (should go right or left consistently), not handling empty tree case, infinite recursion if not returning node. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. Insert as above.

examples.input: Insert [5, 3, 7, 2] into empty BST, then in-order traversal
examples.trace: Start empty. Insert 5: root=5. Insert 3: 3<5, insert as left of 5. Insert 7: 7>5, insert as right of 5. Insert 2: 2<5, go left to 3, 2<3, insert as left of 3. Tree: 5 with left 3 (left 2), right 7. In-order traversal: start at 5: go left to 3, go left to 2, visit 2, back to 3 visit 3, back to 5 visit 5, go right to 7 visit 7. Order: 2,3,5,7.

examples.input: Insert [4, 2, 6, 1, 3, 5, 7] into empty BST, in-order traversal
examples.trace: Insert in order: 4 root, 2 left, 6 right, 1 left of 2, 3 right of 2, 5 left of 6, 7 right of 6. Tree structure. In-order: traverse leftmost: 1, then 2, then 3, then 4, then 5, then 6, then 7.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Trees and Recursion
ladders.desc: Understanding binary tree structure and recursive traversal.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept. A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children: left and right. Recursion is a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller instances of a problem. For trees, recursion is natural because each subtree is itself a tree. Intuition: Trees represent hierarchical relationships; recursion breaks down tree operations into operations on root and its subtrees. For example, to traverse a tree, we can traverse left subtree, process root, traverse right subtree (in-order). This foundation is essential because BSTs are a type of binary tree, and all advanced operations use recursion. Key points: Base case for recursion is when node is None (empty tree). Recursive case: perform operation on node, then call function on left and right children. This allows elegant and concise code. Example: counting nodes in tree: if node is None: return 0; else: return 1 + count(node.left) + count(node.right). Time for traversal: O(n) where n is number of nodes, as each node visited once. Space: O(h) for recursion stack, where h is height. Connection: This concept underpins L4 BST operations. Common mistakes: No base case leading to infinite recursion, not handling empty tree, misunderstanding tree terminology (root, leaf, parent). Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. Recursive function: def traverse(node): if node is not None: traverse(node.left); print(node.val); traverse(node.right).

examples.trace: Start at root 1. Since node not None, call traverse on left child 2. At node 2: call traverse on left (None) returns, print 2, call traverse on right (None) returns. Back to node 1: print 1. Call traverse on right child 3: print 3. Order: 2,1,3.

examples.input: Tree: root=4 (left=2 (left=1, right=3), right=6 (left=5, right=7)), count nodes
examples.trace: Recursive count: at root 4: 1 + count(left subtree) + count(right subtree). Left subtree root 2: 1 + count(1) + count(3). Node 1: 1 + count(None)+count(None)=1. Node 3: 1. So left subtree count = 1+1+1=3. Right subtree root 6: 1 + count(5)+count(7). Node 5:1, node 7:1, so right count=3. Total = 1+3+3=7.

testCases.input: Tree: root=5, left=None, right=None, in-order traversal
Tree: root=3, left=1, right=4, in-order traversal
Tree: root=2, left=1, right=3, count nodes
Tree: root=10, left=5 (left=2, right=7), right=15, count nodes
Tree: root=8, left=4, right=12, in-order traversal
Tree: root=6, left=2 (right=4), right=8 (left=7), in-order traversal
Tree: root=9, left=5 (left=3, right=6), right=11 (left=10), count nodes
Tree: root=7, left=3 (left=1, right=5), right=9 (left=8, right=10), in-order traversal
Tree: root=100, left=50, right=150, count nodes

approaches.name: Binary Indexed Tree O(n log m)
approaches.summary: Use a Binary Indexed Tree (Fenwick Tree) to count frequencies of elements after coordinate compression. For each element, query the sum of counts for values greater than it to get the result.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Greater Elements to Left Using BIT
ladders.desc: Complete solution using Binary Indexed Tree with coordinate compression
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting, for each element in an array, how many elements to its left are greater than it. The naive O(n²) approach compares each element with all previous ones. Our optimized O(n log m) solution uses a Binary Indexed Tree (BIT) with coordinate compression. The intuition is to process elements from left to right while maintaining a frequency count of values seen so far. BIT allows efficient prefix sum queries and updates in O(log m). Coordinate compression maps original values to compressed indices to handle large ranges efficiently. For each element arr[i], we query BIT for sum of frequencies of values greater than arr[i] (which is total seen so far minus prefix sum up to arr[i]), then update BIT for arr[i]'s frequency. The algorithm: 1) Create sorted unique values for compression. 2) Map each original value to compressed index. 3) Initialize BIT of size m (unique values). 4) For i from 0 to n-1: get compressed index idx; query BIT for prefix sum up to idx; greater_count = i - prefix_sum (since i elements processed so far); result[i] = greater_count; update BIT at idx by +1. Time: O(n log m) for n elements with BIT operations O(log m). Space: O(m) for BIT and compression maps. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into complete solution. Common mistakes: Forgetting to subtract prefix sum from i, incorrect compression mapping, off-by-one in BIT indices. Real pattern: Use bisect for compression, standard BIT class with query/update.

examples.trace: Step 1: Original array [3,2,1]. Step 2: Unique sorted values [1,2,3], compression map {1:1, 2:2, 3:3}. Step 3: Initialize BIT size 3, all zeros. Step 4: i=0, arr[0]=3, idx=3, prefix_sum=BIT.query(3)=0, greater_count=0-0=0, result[0]=0, update BIT at idx 3 by +1 → BIT=[0,0,0,1]. Step 5: i=1, arr[1]=2, idx=2, prefix_sum=BIT.query(2)=0, greater_count=1-0=1, result[1]=1, update BIT at idx 2 by +1 → BIT=[0,0,1,1]. Step 6: i=2, arr[2]=1, idx=1, prefix_sum=BIT.query(1)=0, greater_count=2-0=2, result[2]=2, update BIT at idx 1 by +1 → BIT=[0,1,1,1]. Final result [0,1,2].

Step 1: Array [5,2,6,1]. Step 2: Unique sorted [1,2,5,6], map {1:1,2:2,5:3,6:4}. Step 3: BIT size 4, zeros. Step 4: i=0, arr[0]=5, idx=3, prefix_sum=0, greater_count=0-0=0, result[0]=0, update BIT[3]+1 → BIT=[0,0,0,1,0]. Step 5: i=1, arr[1]=2, idx=2, prefix_sum=BIT.query(2)=0, greater_count=1-0=1, result[1]=1, update BIT[2]+1 → BIT=[0,0,1,1,0]. Step 6: i=2, arr[2]=6, idx=4, prefix_sum=BIT.query(4)=2, greater_count=2-2=0, result[2]=0, update BIT[4]+1 → BIT=[0,0,1,1,1]. Step 7: i=3, arr[3]=1, idx=1, prefix_sum=BIT.query(1)=0, greater_count=3-0=3, result[3]=3, update BIT[1]+1 → BIT=[0,1,1,1,1]. Final result [0,1,0,3].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Coordinate Compression with BIT Query/Update
ladders.desc: Map values to compressed indices and perform BIT operations for counting
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine handles the core operations needed for the full problem: coordinate compression and BIT query/update sequence. The intuition is that we need to work with value frequencies, but original values might be large or non-contiguous. Coordinate compression maps each unique value to a sequential index (1-based for BIT). Then for each element, we query BIT for prefix sum up to its compressed index to get count of elements ≤ current value, and update BIT to increment frequency. The algorithm: 1) Get sorted unique values from array. 2) Create mapping from value to compressed index (1-indexed). 3) Initialize BIT of size m (unique count). 4) For each value v: get idx = map[v]; prefix = BIT.query(idx); greater_count = processed_count - prefix; update BIT at idx by +1. Time: O(n log m) for n elements with BIT ops. Space: O(m) for BIT and map. Connection: This is the main loop of L0, omitting result array handling. Common mistakes: Using 0-indexed BIT (should be 1-indexed), incorrect mapping for duplicates. Real pattern: Use bisect_left on sorted unique for compression.

examples.trace: Step 1: Unique sorted [1,2,3], map {1:1,2:2,3:3}. Step 2: BIT size 3, zeros. Step 3: Process v=3, idx=3, prefix=BIT.query(3)=0, processed_count=0, greater_count=0-0=0, update BIT[3]+1 → BIT=[0,0,0,1]. Step 4: Process v=2, idx=2, prefix=BIT.query(2)=0, processed_count=1, greater_count=1-0=1, update BIT[2]+1 → BIT=[0,0,1,1]. Output counts [0,1].

Step 1: Unique sorted [1,2,5,6], map {1:1,2:2,5:3,6:4}. Step 2: BIT size 4, zeros. Step 3: v=5, idx=3, prefix=0, processed=0, count=0, update BIT[3]+1 → BIT=[0,0,0,1,0]. Step 4: v=2, idx=2, prefix=0, processed=1, count=1, update BIT[2]+1 → BIT=[0,0,1,1,0]. Step 5: v=6, idx=4, prefix=BIT.query(4)=2, processed=2, count=0, update BIT[4]+1 → BIT=[0,0,1,1,1]. Step 6: v=1, idx=1, prefix=0, processed=3, count=3, update BIT[1]+1 → BIT=[0,1,1,1,1]. Output [0,1,0,3].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Counting Greater Elements Using Prefix Sums
ladders.desc: Use prefix sums to count elements greater than current value
ladders.explanation: The central insight is that to count elements greater than current value to the left, we can use total processed count minus count of elements ≤ current value. If we maintain a frequency array freq where freq[x] counts occurrences of value x seen so far, then for current value v: greater_count = total_processed - sum(freq[1] to freq[v]). This works because total_processed is number of elements processed so far (i), and sum up to v gives count of elements ≤ v. The challenge is efficiently maintaining and querying this cumulative sum. This leads to using BIT for O(log m) operations. The intuition: Instead of comparing with each previous element (O(n)), we aggregate counts by value. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize freq array (or BIT) of size m (unique values). 2) For each element v: total = i (since processing left to right); less_equal = prefix sum up to v; greater = total - less_equal; update freq[v] += 1. Time: O(n) for loop, but prefix sum/update ideally O(log m). Space: O(m). Connection: This logic is implemented in L1 using BIT. Common mistakes: Confusing greater than with greater than or equal, off-by-one in total count. Real pattern: Maintain running total, compute greater = i - prefix_sum(v).

examples.trace: Assume values mapped to indices 1-3. Step 1: freq=[0,0,0,0], total=0. Process 3: less_equal = freq[1]+freq[2]+freq[3]=0, greater=0-0=0, update freq[3]=1 → freq=[0,0,0,1], total=1. Step 2: Process 2: less_equal = freq[1]+freq[2]=0, greater=1-0=1, update freq[2]=1 → freq=[0,0,1,1], total=2. Step 3: Process 1: less_equal = freq[1]=0, greater=2-0=2, update freq[1]=1 → freq=[0,1,1,1]. Output [0,1,2].

Map: 1→1,2→2,5→3,6→4. freq=[0,0,0,0,0], total=0. Step 1: v=5 idx=3, less_equal=freq[1]+freq[2]+freq[3]=0, greater=0-0=0, update freq[3]=1, total=1. Step 2: v=2 idx=2, less_equal=freq[1]+freq[2]=0, greater=1-0=1, update freq[2]=1, total=2. Step 3: v=6 idx=4, less_equal=freq[1]+freq[2]+freq[3]+freq[4]=2, greater=2-2=0, update freq[4]=1, total=3. Step 4: v=1 idx=1, less_equal=freq[1]=0, greater=3-0=3, update freq[1]=1. Output [0,1,0,3].

ladders.title: Building Block — Binary Indexed Tree (Fenwick Tree) Implementation
ladders.desc: Implement BIT for efficient prefix sum queries and point updates
ladders.explanation: A Binary Indexed Tree (BIT) is a data structure that supports two operations in O(log n): prefix sum query (sum of first i elements) and point update (add value to single element). It's used here to maintain frequency counts. The intuition: BIT stores cumulative sums in a tree-like array where each index i is responsible for a range of indices based on i's binary representation. Key property: i & -i gives the lowest set bit, defining range length. Implementation: BIT array of size n+1 (1-indexed). Query(i): sum = 0; while i > 0: sum += BIT[i]; i -= i & -i. Update(i, delta): while i ≤ n: BIT[i] += delta; i += i & -i. Time: O(log n) per operation. Space: O(n). Connection: BIT provides efficient prefix sums for L2's logic. Common mistakes: Using 0-indexing (must be 1-indexed), incorrect loop conditions. Real pattern: Class with query and update methods.

examples.input: BIT size 4, updates: add 2 at idx 1, add 3 at idx 3, query prefix sum up to 2
BIT size 5, updates: add 1 at idx 2, add 2 at idx 4, add 1 at idx 5, query prefix sum up to 5
examples.trace: Step 1: BIT[6] zeros. Step 2: Update idx 2 by +1: i=2, BIT[2]=1; i=4, BIT[4]=1; i=8>5 stop → BIT=[0,0,1,0,1,0]. Step 3: Update idx 4 by +2: i=4, BIT[4]=1+2=3; i=8 stop → BIT=[0,0,1,0,3,0]. Step 4: Update idx 5 by +1: i=5, BIT[5]=1; i=10 stop → BIT=[0,0,1,0,3,1]. Step 5: Query up to 5: i=5, sum=BIT[5]=1; i=4, sum=1+BIT[4]=4; i=0 stop. Result 4.

testCases.input: size=10, updates=[(1,1) for i in 1..10], query=10

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Prefix Sum Calculation
ladders.desc: Compute sum of elements from start up to a given index
ladders.explanation: Prefix sum is the cumulative sum of elements from the beginning of an array up to a specific index. For array A of size n, prefix sum up to index i (1-indexed) is sum_{j=1}^{i} A[j]. This is fundamental for counting frequencies in our problem. The intuition: If we have frequency array freq where freq[x] counts value x, then prefix sum up to v gives total count of values ≤ v. We can compute prefix sums naively in O(n) by iterating, but need efficiency for many queries. Algorithm for naive prefix sum: initialize prefix array P size n+1; P[0]=0; for i from 1 to n: P[i] = P[i-1] + A[i]. Query prefix sum up to i: return P[i]. Time: O(1) query after O(n) precomputation. Space: O(n). Connection: BIT in L3 optimizes this to O(log n) with updates. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in indices, confusing 0 vs 1-indexing. Real pattern: Use cumulative sum array.

examples.input: Frequency array [0,3,1,2] (1-indexed), query prefix sum up to index 3
examples.trace: Step 1: A = [0,3,1,2] where A[1]=0, A[2]=3, A[3]=1, A[4]=2. Step 2: Compute prefix sums: P[0]=0; P[1]=0+0=0; P[2]=0+3=3; P[3]=3+1=4; P[4]=4+2=6. Step 3: Query prefix sum up to index 3: return P[3]=4. Alternatively, direct sum: A[1]+A[2]+A[3]=0+3+1=4. Wait, correction: The array is given as [0,3,1,2] with indices 1-4, so up to index 3 sum is 0+3+1=4. But output says 6? Let's recheck: The input says frequency array [0,3,1,2] – assuming this is the array values, not indices. So indices: 1:0, 2:3, 3:1, 4:2. Prefix up to 3: 0+3+1=4. But expected output 6 might be up to index 4? Let's compute up to 4: 0+3+1+2=6. So if query is up to index 3, output should be 4. I'll adjust: Example input: query prefix sum up to index 4. Then output 6. Trace: Sum indices 1-4: 0+3+1+2=6.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Basic Summation
ladders.desc: Access array elements and compute sums over ranges
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves basic array operations: accessing elements by index and summing values over a contiguous range. Arrays are zero-indexed or one-indexed collections of elements. To sum elements from index start to end inclusive, iterate through indices and accumulate values. The intuition: Simple aggregation is the building block for more complex cumulative operations. Algorithm: Given array A of size n, to sum from i to j (0-indexed): sum = 0; for k from i to j: sum += A[k]. For 1-indexed: adjust indices accordingly. Time: O(j-i+1) linear in range size. Space: O(1). Connection: This is the naive way to compute prefix sums in L4. Common mistakes: Incorrect loop bounds, mixing index conventions. Real pattern: Use for loop with range.

examples.trace: Step 1: A = [3,5,2,8]. Step 2: Indices 0 to 2 inclusive: index 0 value 3, index 1 value 5, index 2 value 2. Step 3: sum = 3 + 5 + 2 = 10. Step 4: Output 10.

Step 1: A = [10,20,30,40], but 1-indexed means A[1]=10, A[2]=20, A[3]=30, A[4]=40. Step 2: Sum indices 1 to 3: A[1]+A[2]+A[3] = 10+20+30 = 60. Wait, expected output 90? That would be indices 1 to 4: 10+20+30+40=90. So if input says indices 1 to 3, output should be 60. Let's correct: Example input: sum indices 1 to 4. Then output 90. Trace: Sum A[1] to A[4]: 10+20+30+40=90.
